pdf_checks.name: first_two_pages_thesis
needles: Its thesis is that secure edge storage

scope: source anchors for pressure figure/table, gap, thesis, contributions, and component-stack answer
supporting paper-spine, hero-result, novelty-isolation, and accepted-structure gates

source_anchors.name: pressure_result
source_anchors.text: Figure~\ref{fig:first_page_qos} shows the retained first-page pressure result. Mounted secure-storage pressure raises foreground SQLite p99 from 6.4~ms to 13.8~ms and introduces one 10~ms deadline miss. AEGIS-Q marks foreground files latency-sensitive, throttles only elastic background writes, and recovers to 8.8~ms p99 with zero misses while retaining 2.7~MB/s of background progress. This result motivates the paper's spine: policy must be visible at the secure-storage layer, but persistent format correctness must remain independent of the accelerator.

source_anchors.name: capability_table
source_anchors.text: \caption{Capability comparison that defines AEGIS-Q's design gap. ``Policy'' means storage-visible accelerator/QoS/freshness policy, not implementation maturity.}

source_anchors.name: concrete_gap
source_anchors.text: Post-quantum key establishment is relevant to long-lived edge data, but it does not replace symmetric storage encryption. ML-KEM establishes shared secrets, whereas AES-GCM remains the appropriate mechanism for bulk data records~\cite{mlkem}. SHA-256 can bind committed-prefix anchor material to persisted metadata~\cite{sha256}. The systems challenge is therefore to compose ordinary storage invariants---a key hierarchy that survives remount, nonce/generation state that remains safe across crashes, ciphertext durability before metadata publication, and fail-closed recovery---with accelerator placement and shared-resource control. Table~\ref{tab:capability_matrix} positions that gap against plaintext, gocryptfs, fscrypt, dm-crypt, fs-verity/dm-integrity, TPM/TEE-backed designs, and GPU-storage systems~\cite{fscrypt, dmcrypt, gocryptfs, optee}.

source_anchors.name: central_thesis
source_anchors.text: AEGIS-Q explores this gap as a UMA-aware secure-storage runtime. Its thesis is that secure edge storage should expose one durable authenticated format while separating three operational planes: a CPU-first data plane for latency-sensitive AES-GCM writes, an elastic GPU lane for large independent cryptographic batches, and an external-anchor path that can fail closed when replayed state no longer matches protected freshness state. The claim is stronger than a microbenchmark-only story because it ties placement decisions to format invariants and recovery behavior.

source_anchors.name: design_insight
source_anchors.text: The key design insight is that accelerator placement must be subordinate to storage correctness. AEGIS-Q never lets the GPU define the persistent record format. Data records are AES-GCM blocks bound to file identifier, logical block, generation, and plaintext length. Metadata publication follows a data/journal/checkpoint order. Optional GPU work either produces format-compatible results or falls back to the CPU path. Jobs without producer-supplied slack are routed conservatively. This makes the accelerator an execution choice, not a new security boundary.

source_anchors.name: contribution_c1
source_anchors.text: \item \textbf{C1: Secure-storage format and placement boundary.} AEGIS-Q implements authenticated per-file envelopes, generation-bound AEAD context, durable mapping publication, and explicit CPU/GPU placement rules. Design/evaluation: Section~\ref{sec:design_pipeline} and Section~\ref{sec:eval_workloads}.

source_anchors.name: contribution_c2
source_anchors.text: \item \textbf{C2: Scoped placement and workflow evidence on Thor.} Retained diagnostic primitive runs and a mounted envelope-refresh workflow explain the CPU-first data lane and optional batched GPU lane. Design/evaluation: Section~\ref{sec:design_uma} and Section~\ref{sec:eval_performance}.

source_anchors.name: contribution_c3
source_anchors.text: \item \textbf{C3: Recovery/freshness evidence with failure labels.} The repository retains clean-remount, tamper-rejection, file-anchor negative-control, TPM replay-after-advance, SQLite/\texttt{dbm.dumb} stale replay, and SQLite syscall-exact app-crash evidence. Design/evaluation: Section~\ref{sec:design_security} and Section~\ref{sec:eval_workloads}.

source_anchors.name: contribution_c4
source_anchors.text: \item \textbf{C4: Telemetry/QoS evidence.} AEGIS-Q includes deterministic admission traces, same-run \texttt{tegrastats}/CUPTI-to-mounted-FUSE throttling, and the mounted SQLite recovery bundle in Figure~\ref{fig:first_page_qos}. Design/evaluation: Section~\ref{sec:design_uma} and Section~\ref{sec:eval_qos}.

source_anchors.name: not_weaker_component_stack
source_anchors.text: The value of AEGIS-Q is not that user-space encryption is inherently preferable to kernel encryption. In fact, kernel-integrated systems have a clear maturity advantage. AEGIS-Q is also not merely gocryptfs/fscrypt plus CUDA/TPM scripts: the research question is whether one mounted runtime can keep authenticated-block publication, storage-visible QoS/admission, optional batched GPU key-plane work, and external freshness checks under one evidence contract. In that setting, the storage layer exposes policy decisions that conventional encryption layers usually hide. A FUSE daemon can decide whether a particular maintenance job is latency-sensitive, batch-shaped, or freshness-critical; it can also fail closed before exposing a file whose envelope, checkpoint, or external anchor is inconsistent.
